coredata.dc:title: Clusterization-triggered emission artificial enzyme (CTEzyme) with specific activity inhibition boosting ratiometric fluorescence detection of pesticide
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Yanfang
Chen, Haiying
Chang, Chao
Xu, Lubin
Han, Lei
coredata.dc:description: The extensive lack of specific recognition elements seriously limits the specificity of pesticide assay methods. In typical ratiometric fluorescence assays, the interdependence of the two fluorescence signals can compromise self-validation and reliability. Here, fluorescence nanoparticles with the diameter of 2035 nm were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using levodopa, Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane, and copper chloride as precursors. These nanoparticles exhibited typical clusterization-triggered emission (CTE) characteristics and inherent peroxidase-like activity, which were named as “CTEzyme”. Exhilaratingly, methidathion (MD), a broad-spectrum organophosphorus pesticide, could not only specifically inhibit peroxidase-like activity of CTEzyme, reducing the fluorescence of diaminophenazine at 555 nm, but also enhance the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect of CTEzyme, amplifying the fluorescence of CTEzyme at 375 nm. Therefore, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on CTEzyme as specific recognition probe for MD detection was successfully constructed with ultra-low limit of detection (0.625 µg L−1), high selectivity, and acceptable reliability for real sample assay. More importantly, both fluorescence signals were directly triggered by the target (MD), in clear contrast to common ratiometric fluorescence assays where the second signal is typically dependent on a quenching effect related to the first signal. The dependent signals enabled self-validation, minimizing the misjudgment risk. Additionally, the second signal at 375 nm was enhanced by MD due to AIE effect, significantly increase the sensitivity. The discovery of CTEzyme provides a specific fluorescent probe for pesticide assays, and would foster advancements in the development and the application of CTEgens by endowing CTEgens with enzyme-like activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Clusterization-triggered emission
Aggregation-induced emission
Artificial enzyme
Nanozyme
Fluorescence assay
Pesticide detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: MD detection in real water samples
Synthesis and characterization of Cu-dopped PDs (CTEzymes)
MD-caused fluorescence changes in reaction system based on CTEzyme

ZR2024YQ026 and ZR2021MB018), Research Foundation for Distinguished Scholars of Qingdao Agricultural University (No. 663\u20131117015). We are grateful to staff from the Instrumental Analysis Center of Qingdao Agricultural University for providing valuable help.

ZR2024YQ026 and ZR2021MB018), Research Foundation for Distinguished Scholars of Qingdao Agricultural University (No. 663-1117015). We are grateful to staff from the Instrumental Analysis Center of Qingdao Agricultural University for providing valuable help.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and morphological characterization of CTEzyme. (a) Synthesis schematic of CTEzyme and its photographs after freeze-drying under visible light and 365 nm UV lamp. (b, c) TEM images of CTEzyme. (d) Particle size distribution of CTEzyme.

Composition characterization of CTEzyme. (a) XPS spectrum of CTEzyme. High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1 s, (c) O 1 s, and (d) Cu2p. (e) FTIR spectra of CTEzyme. (f) 1H NMR of the CTEzyme in DMSO-D6.

Fluorescence characteristics of CTEzyme. (a) UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence excitation spectrum of CTEzyme. (b) The fluorescence emission spectra of CTEzyme under various excitation wavelengths. (c) The corresponding relationship of emission peak and excitation peak. (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of CTEzyme aqueous solution with different concentrations. (e) The relationship of CTEzyme concentration and fluorescence intensity. (f) Fluorescence emission spectra of CTEzyme in water/THF mixture solutions with different THF fractions.

Peroxidase-like activity verification of CTEzyme. (a) The catalytic activity schematic of CTEzyme. Absorbance spectra of (b) TMB, (c) ABTS and (d) OPD oxidation in various reaction solutions. Absorbance spectra of (e) TMB, (f) ABTS and (g) OPD with H2O2 in the presence of CTEzyme, PDs or Cu2+ ions.

The MD-regulated fluorescence changes of CTEzyme. (a) Fluorescence spectra of different CTEzyme-catalyzed reactions with H2O2. (b) Fluorescence spectra and (c) Fluorescence lifetime of CTEzyme with or without MD. (d) Fluorescence spectra of CTEzyme with the addition of OPD, DAP or MD. (e) Zeta potentials of CTEzyme incubated with OPD and DAP. (f) Schematic diagram of the relationship among CTEzyme, MD, OPD and DAP.

Ratiometric fluorescence detection of MD. (a) Schematic diagram of the detection principle. (b) Fluorescence spectra of detection system with different concentrations of MD (0, 2, 5, 7.5, 10, 15, 20, 30, and 50 μg L−1). (c) The calibration curve of MD. (d) The chemical structures of MD and other pesticides. (e) The selectivity assay of MD detection method to various pesticides (MD, 50 μg/L; other pesticides, 0.1 mg/mL).

head.ce:title: Clusterization-triggered emission artificial enzyme (CTEzyme) with specific activity inhibition boosting ratiometric fluorescence detection of pesticide

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, 700 Changcheng Road, Qingdao, Shandong 266109, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, 700 Changcheng Road, Qingdao 266109, Shandong, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Y. Zhao and H. Chen contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The extensive lack of specific recognition elements seriously limits the specificity of pesticide assay methods. In typical ratiometric fluorescence assays, the interdependence of the two fluorescence signals can compromise self-validation and reliability. Here, fluorescence nanoparticles with the diameter of 2035 nm were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using levodopa, Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane, and copper chloride as precursors. These nanoparticles exhibited typical clusterization-triggered emission (CTE) characteristics and inherent peroxidase-like activity, which were named as “CTEzyme”. Exhilaratingly, methidathion (MD), a broad-spectrum organophosphorus pesticide, could not only specifically inhibit peroxidase-like activity of CTEzyme, reducing the fluorescence of diaminophenazine at 555 nm, but also enhance the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect of CTEzyme, amplifying the fluorescence of CTEzyme at 375 nm. Therefore, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on CTEzyme as specific recognition probe for MD detection was successfully constructed with ultra-low limit of detection (0.625 µg L−1), high selectivity, and acceptable reliability for real sample assay. More importantly, both fluorescence signals were directly triggered by the target (MD), in clear contrast to common ratiometric fluorescence assays where the second signal is typically dependent on a quenching effect related to the first signal. The dependent signals enabled self-validation, minimizing the misjudgment risk. Additionally, the second signal at 375 nm was enhanced by MD due to AIE effect, significantly increase the sensitivity. The discovery of CTEzyme provides a specific fluorescent probe for pesticide assays, and would foster advancements in the development and the application of CTEgens by endowing CTEgens with enzyme-like activity.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CTEzyme, artificial enzyme with CTE effect, was designed as specific probe of methidathion for the ratiometric fluorescent assay with high selectivity, high sensitivity and self-validation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the development of the specific detection of pesticides has been attracting much attention, due to the disturbances of other pesticides and emerging contaminants [1–3]. Among various assay approaches for organophosphorus pesticides (such as electrochemical sensor [4], high performance thin-layer chromatography [5], and gas chromatography [6]), the fluorescent method have shown its unique advantages including easy operation, simplicity and high sensitivity [7,8]. However, most of fluorescent methods generally lack specificity because of the lack of specific recognition elements (such as antibodies and enzymes). Although some enzymes (such as acetylcholinesterase) can serve as recognition elements for detection based on activity inhibition, their lack of selectivity towards pesticides restricts them within broad-spectrum determination rather than specific detection [9]. Although some artificial enzymes, particularly nanozymes, exhibit many distinct advantages over biological enzymes, such as low cost, ease of large-scale preparation, and high stability under several environments [10,11]. Most of cases about nanozyme-based detection of organophosphorus pesticides rely on enzyme inhibition effect, which neither excludes the use of enzymes nor achieves high selectivity [12,13]. Furthermore, high selectivity often requires antibodies, increasing the costs [14]. Therefore, it is challenging to achieve the specific pesticide detection without enzymes or antibodies.

To enhance the sensitivity, self-correction and self-validation of fluorescent method, the ratiometric strategies based on nanozymes are continuously being developed by recording two fluorescent signals [15–17]. However, in the typical ratiometric fluorescence assay for pesticides, both the two signals are not severally caused by detection objects directly. For instance, nanozymes can catalyze the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to fluorescence oxide (oxOPD), which can quench the fluorescence of additional nanomaterials by the internal filtering effect and the fluorescence resonance energy transfer [18–20]. Meanwhile, detection objects can directly induce the destruction of nanozyme and then the reduction in oxOPD fluorescence. In other words, the first signal (the fluorescence of oxOPD) is directly derived from detection objects, but the second signal (the fluorescence of quantum dots) is directly derived from the first signal. Therefore, the both fluorescence signal exhibits a dependency relationship rather than independency relationship, in order that self-validation of two signals cannot be realized in the real sense. It is challenging and interesting to achieve the direct triggering of target to two signals for ratiometric fluorescence assay.

As the popular fluorescent materials, the organic luminescent materials can emit fluorescence in virtue of their large conjugated structures or chromophores [21]. However, they generally encounter “aggregation-caused quenching” (ACQ) phenomenon in high concentration or solid state because of the intermolecular π-π stacking effect, which hinders their applications in aqueous solution and physiological environment [22,23]. Fortunately, aggregation-induced emission (AIE) luminogens (AIEgens) were discovered by Tang’s group to demonstrate an AIE effect, which is an opposite optical phenomenon against ACQ [24,25]. The luminescence features of AIEgens in the aggregated state demonstrate enormous advantages on fluorescent assay in aqueous solutions and physiological environments [20,26]. However, the lack of specific recognition function makes AIEgens rarely used in the specific pesticide- detection. Therefore, it will be interesting to directly develop AIEgens with specific recognition function.

As a unique member of the AIEgens family, clusterization-triggered emission (CTE) luminogens (CTEgens) with no conjugated structures or well-defined chromophores also exhibit high emission efficiency [27], such as saccharide [28], aliphatic polymer [29], polymer dots (PDs) [30] and poly (vinyl amine) [31]. CTEgens can serve as indicators or sensors of external stimuli, because any stimulus that can change the aggregation state of the CTEgens would lead to a corresponding response on its fluorescence [32]. Due to high photoluminescence quantum yield, low cost, good water solubility and biocompatibility, CTEgens have been widely used in bioimaging and fluorescent sensor [33,34]. However, there is rare application of CTEgens in pesticide detection. An abundance of evidences suggests that the exploration of AIEgens with distinctive properties holds great potential for novel analytical applications [20,35]. In our previous work, AIEgens with enzyme-like activity (AIEzyme) were designed, which demonstrates the superiority of nanomaterials with both AIE property and enzyme-like activity [36]. Considering the reported CTEgens generally lack enzyme-like activity, the development CTEgens with enzyme-like activity is expected to greatly expanded the application scope of CTEgens.

In this work, we synthesized Cu-dopped PDs by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Interestingly, Cu-dopped PDs exhibited not only CTE effect, but also peroxidase-like activity. So, we called this new luminophore as “CTEzyme”, i.e. “CTEgens with enzyme-like activity” or “artificial enzyme with CTE effect”. Exhilaratingly, methidathion (MD), a common broad-spectrum organophosphorus pesticide, was found to specifically inhibit peroxidase-like activity of CTEzyme, thereby preventing the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to fluorescent product (2,3-diaminophenazine, DAP), which emits at 555 nm. On the other hand, the strong binding affinity between MD and CTEzyme significantly enhanced the inherent fluorescence of CTEzyme at 375 nm, due to the AIE effect. Accordingly, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I375/I555) increased with the concentration of MD, leading to the successful construction of a novel ratiometric fluorescent method for MD detection. Therefore, this work improves the self-validation capability of the ratiometric fluorescent method. The two signals were caused directly by MD, resulting in the independence of the two signals. The introduction of CTE theory provides a solid explanation for the effects of OPD, DAP and MD on fluorescence changes of PDs. The discovery of CTEzyme not only expands the tool kits of CTEgens and nanozymes, but also provides a specific fluorescent probe for pesticide assay.

Copper chloride (7.5 mM) and levodopa (1.25 mM) was mixed into Tris aqueous solution (37.5 mM), and then reacted by a hydrothermal method at 180 °C for 4 hours. The resultant solution was filtered with a microporous filter (0.22 μm) and centrifuged at 10,000 r/min to remove the large particles. The supernatant was dialyzed with a dialysis membrane (MWCO, 1000 Da) for 24 h. Then, the CTEzyme solid was prepared by freeze-drying for further use.

To check the peroxidase-like feature of CTEzyme, the chromogenic reactions were performed with TMB, ABTS and OPD as chromogenic substrates in the presence of H2O2. In brief, CTEzyme (0.125 mg L−1) solution, chromogenic substrates and H2O2 were taken into 96-wellplate. After incubating at ambient temperature (25 °C) for 20 minutes, the absorbance spectra were recorded by microplate reader to evaluate the evolution degree of reaction system. In addition, the reaction systems without H2O2 or CTEzyme were set as control groups. For comparison, PDs and Cu2+ with the same amount were also used as the catalysts for the oxidation of the chromogenic substrates under the same conditions.

50 μL of CTEzyme solution (0.5 mg L−1) and 100 μL of MD solution with different concentration were mixed in the centrifugal tube for 1 minute, and then 750 μL of H2O2 (5 mM) aqueous solution and 100 μL of OPD aqueous solution (0.5 mg mL−1) were added into the above mixture. The mixture solution was incubated for 20 min at room temperature (25 °C). Then, the fluorescence intensities at 375 nm and 555 nm were recorded by fluorescence spectrometer. The width of the slit is 5 × 5 nm and the detection excitation wavelength is 330 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MD detection in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water and lake water was from Qingdao Agricultural University, and river water was from a local river. The water samples were pretreated by 0.22 µm filter membrane to remove the solid impurities. For recovery experiments, diﬀerent concentrations of MD were separately added in the lake water samples. 50 μL of CTEzyme solution (0.5 mg L−1) and 100 μL of water samples were mixed in the centrifugal tube for 1 minute. And then, 750 μL of H2O2 (5 mM) aqueous solution and 100 μL of OPD aqueous solution (0.5 mg mL−1) were added into the above mixture. The fluorescence value was determined after 20 minutes, and the concentration value was calculated by the fluorescence intensity ratio (I375/I555).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Cu-dopped PDs (CTEzymes)
ce:section.ce:para: At the beginning of work, Cu-dopped polymer dots was facilely prepared by a one-pot solvothermal approach under 180 °C using copper chloride, levodopa and Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris) as precursors ( Fig. 1a). The basis of synthesis is the self-polymerization of levodopa, a kind of small neurotransmitter molecule without chromophores [37]. The freeze-dried solid powder product (CTEzyme) exhibited fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp, demonstrating the AIE effect (Fig. 1a). CTEzyme could be excited at 330 nm and emitted at 375 nm (Fig. S1). Interestingly, we found that the fluorescence intensity of the product with Tris was significantly higher than one without Tris (Fig. S1), possibly because the alkaline environment provided by Tris is conducive to the self-polymerization of levodopa [38]. Moreover, Tris can promote the adsorption of copper ions, and incorporate more N into the product during carbonization for enhancing fluorescence [39]. In addition, the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) diagrams showed that CTEzymes were nanoparticles (Fig. 1b, c) with the size of 2035 nm (Fig. 1d), suggesting as-prepared CTEzymes were not carbon quantum dots, because the size of carbon quantum dots was within 10 nm [40]. In addition, the effect of Cu-doping amount on the morphology of the synthesized CTEzymes was investigated by TEM images (Fig. S2). While the size of the materials remained relatively stable, the dispersibility was seemingly affected by Cu2+ concentrations, which may influence the fluorescence response in subsequent detection application.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was further employed to identify the chemical valence state and surface composition of the CTEzyme structure. As depicted in Fig. 2a, the XPS spectra confirmed the presence of the C, N, O, and Cu elements. The high-resolution C1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into three peaks corresponding to the -C-C (284.8 eV), -C-N (286.1 eV) and OC-O (289 eV) (Fig. 2b). The N1s spectrum revealed two characteristic peaks (Fig. S3), which correspond to the tertiary amine (399.5 eV) and -N-H (401.3 eV). In Fig. 2c, the binding energies of O1s at 531.5 and 533 eV correspond to CO and C-O-H, respectively. The Cu 2p spectrum showed typical Cu 2p1/2 located at 952.5 eV and Cu 2p3/2 located at 932.5 eV (Fig. 2d). It could be clearly observed that there was no accompanying peak between 952.5 eV and 932.5 eV, which indicated that the valence state of copper was univalence [41].

Subsequently, the structure and the surface groups of CTEzyme were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). As shown in Fig. 2e, the broad vibration band around 31503450 cm−1 was ascribed to O-H and N-H stretching vibration, and the peaks at 1610 cm−1 and 13801450 cm−1 were the skeleton vibration of CO and C-N correspondingly [42]. Moreover, the characteristic absorption peaks at 1125, 1050, and 780 cm−1 were attributed to C-O-C stretching vibration, N-Cu stretching vibration, and O-H bending vibration, respectively [43]. It was clear that Cu was indeed doped in CTEzyme. These FTIR results were tally with XPS analysis.

Additionally, 1H NMR was employed to distinguish CTEzymes and carbon quantum dots (Fig. 2f). Deuterium-labeled DMSO-D6 was employed as a solvent. The peaks at 1.19, 2.51, 3.4, 3.67, and 4.26 ppm corresponded to CH3, DMSO-D6, water, CH2, and olefinic hydrogen respectively. It was explicitly demonstrated that there was no peak at 116 ppm, indicating the absence of the graphene structure and ruling out the possibility of carbon quantum dots. So, CTEzymes had no graphene structure and were not carbon quantum dots.

According to the above results, the as-prepared CTEzyme does neither possess typical chromophores and graphene structures, nor belong to typical carbon quantum dots. Considering CTE luminogens have non-conjugated molecular structures [44], we hypothesize that the bright fluorescence emission of AIEzyme may be attributed to the CTE effect. To confirm this hypothesis, the standard features of CTEgens need to be researched for CTEzyme. Compared with traditional luminophores, CTEgens exhibit many similarities and characteristics. Numerous studies have demonstrated that CTEgens generally possess the following characteristic features [32]. (1) The excitation spectra of CTEgens are redshifted in comparison with their absorption spectra. (2) Their emission wavelengths are dependent on excitation wavelength, where longer wavelength of excitation will generate a red-shifted fluorescence emission. (3) They show the classic AIE effect, where they have non or weak luminescence in dilute solution but bright emission in high concentration or aggregated state. So, these above features are investigated as the judgment basis to prove the CTE effect of CTEzyme as follow.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CTEzymes ( Fig. 3a) exhibited a strong absorption peak within 200300 nm, which corresponded to the π-π* transition produced by aromatic rings and the n-π* transition of CO [45]. Meanwhile, its fluorescence excitation spectrum was also investigated. Obviously, the excitation spectra of clusteroluminogens appear redshifted against their absorption spectra. In other words, AIEzyme could be excited by light whose wavelength was beyond the range of strong absorption peaks. This phenomenon can be seen as a general feature of CTEgens [32].

In addition, fluorescence emission spectra were also investigated under various excitation wavelengths (Fig. 3b). When the excitation wavelength increased from 300 nm to 380 nm, the emission wavelength of CTEzyme appeared redshift from 368 nm to 412 nm (44 nm of redshift), indicating that CTEzyme exhibited excitation-wavelength-dependent luminescence property (Fig. 3c and S4), which is another classical feature of CTEgens.[32] Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity was highest under 330 nm of excitation wavelength (Fig. S5), which was used for follow-up experiments in order to obtain high sensitivity.

The AIE effect of CTEzyme were verified by gradually increasing the concentration of CTEzyme from 0.025 to 0.75 mg mL−1 and testing the fluorescence intensity of the aqueous solution at 330 nm of excitation wavelength (Figs. 1a and 3d). CTEzymes showed weak ﬂuorescence in the low concentration, but strong emissions in the increased concentration. Moreover, a linear relationship could be observed between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of CTEzyme (Fig. 3e), suggesting the concentration of CTEzyme can be quantitatively determined from its fluorescence intensity without weighing. This concentration-enhanced fluorescence of CTEzyme accord with AIE effect, which was different to the ACQ phenomenon of traditional fluorescent dyes [46].

AIE effect has been further confirmed by observing the AIE phenomenon in poor solvents. Considering CTEzyme prepared from levodopa and Tris has the abundant amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface (Fig. 2e), which make CTEzyme have relatively highwater solubility, THF was selected as the poor solvent and H2O was selected as the good solvent. Then, the fluorescence of CTEzyme in H2O/THF mixed solution at different THF contents was observed (Fig. 3f). When the THF fraction increased from 0 % to 90 %, the fluorescence intensity of the CTEzyme increased again. Due to the addition of the poor solvent, the solubility of CTEzymes gradually decreased, thereby resulting in the restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM) [20]. As a result, the energy loss caused by non-radiative transition was reduced, and the fluorescence intensity was further enhanced [47]. Moreover, a linear relationship could be observed between the fluorescence intensity and the THF fraction (Fig. S6), suggesting the water or THF content can be quantitatively determined from its fluorescence intensity without other methods.

The peroxidase-like activity of CTEzyme was investigated by typical peroxidase substrates TMB, ABTS and OPD ( Fig. 4a). As expected, the CTEzyme could catalyze the oxidation of the colorless TMB to blue product (oxTMB) in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (Fig. 4a), exhibiting an absorption peak at 650 nm (Fig. 4b). In the absence of H2O2, TMB could not be oxidized under the catalysis of CTEzyme, indicating that CTEzyme did not possess oxidase-like activity. Similarly, the CTEzyme could catalyze the oxidation of ABTS and OPD to green and yellow product (oxABTS and oxOPD) in the presence of H2O2, while the negligible absorbance was achieved in the absence of H2O2. (Fig. 4b-d). These results confirmed CTEzyme possessed inherent peroxidase-like activity, but not oxidase-like activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MD-caused fluorescence changes in reaction system based on CTEzyme
ce:section.ce:para: Here, we were excited to find that MD could alter the fluorescence of the OPD oxidation reaction system catalyzed by CTEzyme ( Fig. 5a). In this system, there are two fluorescence peaks, one from the characteristic peak of CTEzyme at 375 nm and one from the characteristic peak of DAP at 555 nm. There was no obvious fluorescence change of CTEzyme at 375 nm in the presence of either OPD or H2O2. When OPD and H2O2 coexist in the reaction system, CTEzyme can catalyze OPD to rapidly produce DAP, which generate the fluorescence peak at 555 nm. After sequentially adding MD, the emission peak at 375 nm would be enhanced substantially because of the AIE effect. It's worth noting that MD could inhibit the catalytic efficiency of CTEzyme and hence the generation of DAP, decreasing the fluorescence intensity at 555 nm. And, the fluorescence intensity remains nearly unchanged regardless of whether CTEzyme reacts with MD or not (Fig. 5b). Consequently, MD cannot directly affect the fluorescence characteristics of CTEzyme. The fluorescence lifetimes of CTEzyme in the absence and presence of MD were measured to be 6.88 ns and 6.87 ns, respectively (Fig. 5c), indicated that there was no obvious electron or energy transfer between CTEzyme and MD.

This phenomenon was further studied as is shown in Fig. 5d. When CTEzyme was incubated with MD or MD + OPD without H2O2, no fluorescence signal was changed, compared with individual CTEzyme. In contrast, DAP, the oxide of OPD, could slightly elevate the characteristic peak of CTEzyme, because the affinity between DAP and CTEzyme caused RIM effect and AIE effect [20]. In order to further confirm the affinity, the relevant Zeta potentials were measured (Fig. 5e). OPD cannot obviously change the Zeta potential of CTEzyme, but DAP can obviously change the Zeta potential of CTEzyme from negative values to positive values, suggesting that DAP could be adsorbed on CTEzyme by the electrostatic interaction.

Over the past 20 years, research into AIE and CTE has made great advances in various fields including biological imaging, medical diagnosis, and so on [48,49]. These achievements have helped researchers to explain many luminescence phenomena from the perspective of aggregates [24,50]. In this study, the fluorescence response of CTEzyme to MD can be well explained by CTE and AIE effects. Specifically, the characteristic fluorescence peak of CTEzyme only increases in the presence of DAP, MD and CTEzyme together. This observation suggests that the simultaneous existence of DAP, MD and CTEzyme facilitates the formation and stabilization of the clusters, which can restrict the intramolecular motions to reduce the nonradiative decay, and then activate the AIE and CTE effects to enhance the CTE fluorescence (Fig. 5f) [50]. This work offers new insights into the mechanism of fluorescence response of PDs to analytes.

As a broad-spectrum organophosphorus pesticide, MD is commonly utilized to control agricultural pests [51]. On account of the large-scale seeding of MD-resistant crops, the application scope of MD has been continuously expanded [52], leading to the widespread presence of MD residues in soil and water environments [41,53]. However, it is classified as a Class I toxic substance by the US Environmental Protection Agency, because it can pose a serious threat to the ecological environment [54,55]. Furthermore, MD is difficult to degrade and transform in a short period of time after entering the water environment, causing adverse effects to aquatic species and human beings even in low concentrations [56]. Therefore, it is necessary to establish sensitive, specific and convenient detection method for MD.

Give the findings of the above phenomena, a ratiometric fluorescence assay strategy of MD was proposed ( Fig. 6a). In the CTEzyme-catalyzed reaction system, there are two fluorescence peaks, the characteristic peak of CTEzyme at 375 nm and the characteristic peak of DAP at 555 nm. MD can inhibit the peroxidase-like activity of CTEzyme, reducing the fluorescence intensity of DAP at 555 nm. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of CTEzyme at 375 nm drastically enhanced due to the CTE effect and RIM effect [20]. Accordingly, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I375/I555) increased with the concentration of MD, which can be used for MD detection (Fig. 6a).

To improve the sensitivity of CTEzyme-based fluorescence assay, the potential influencing factors were systematically optimized. CTEzymes with different Cu-doping amounts were applied on the assay method. As shown in Fig. S9a, with increasing Cu-doping amount, the fluorescence signal ratio (I375/I555) progressively increased to a maximum before declining. The optimal Cu-doping concentration was 7.5 mM, which was utilized for subsequent experiments. In addition, the influence of CTEzyme concentration and reaction time on assay performance was investigated. Fig. S9b exhibited that the increase of CTEzyme concentration caused a gradual rise in I375/I555 to increase firstly, followed by a plateau at 0.5 mg/L. Similarly, the fluorescence response was time-dependent and stabilized after 20 minutes (Fig. S9c). Therefore, a CTEzyme concentration of 0.5 mg/L and a reaction time of 20 min were used in subsequent experiments.

After a series of condition optimization experiments (Fig. S9), the proposed strategy was used for the detection of MD. As shown in Fig. 6b, the fluorescence intensity at 375 nm and 555 nm showed a uniform change with MD concentration, and the values of I375/I555 were linearly correlated with the concentration of MD (Fig. 6c). The linear equation was y = 0.126x + 1.242 (R2 = 0.993) with linear range of 250 μg L−1, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.625 μg L−1 (0.4 nM) that was 13 orders of magnitude lower to the reported methods including electrochemical sensor [51,57], high performance liquid chromatography [58], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [59] and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [60] (Table S1).

In conventional nanozyme-based ratiometric fluorescence assays, both fluorescence signals are not directly derived from the target analyte. Instead, only the primary signal stemmed from the analyte itself, while the secondary signal was directly derived from the primary signal [18,20,61]. For example, Su’s group utilized peroxidase-like Fe-single atom nanozymes (Fe-SAzyme) and fluorescent Cu nanoclusters to realize the ratiometric assay of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) [61]. Fe-SAzyme could catalyze the oxidation of OPD into the fluorescent product DAP, and then DAP subsequently quenched the fluorescence of Cu nanoclusters via fluorescence resonance energy transfer. So, AChE-catalyzed hydrolysate thiocholine could inhibit the activity of Fe-single atom nanozymes, generating the first signal, the fluorescence change of DAP. And then, the fluorescence change of DAP caused the second signal, the fluorescence change of Cu nanocluster [61]. Therefore, the second fluorescence signal depended on the first signal, which was not conducive to the mutual verification of the two signals. Moreover, the second fluorescence signal could only be recovered, but not further enhanced, resulting in the limited sensitivity. In this work, we successfully developed a ratiometric fluorescence sensor with two independent fluorescent signals. The both signals were directly caused by target object MD, achieving the self-verification function. Moreover, the second signal (fluorescent change of CTEzyme) could be enhanced by MD due to AIE effect, significantly increase the sensitivity.

To further investigate the selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescence MD analysis method based on CTEzymes, different types of pesticides in high concentration (0.1 mg mL−1) were substituted for MD (50 μg L−1), such as diclofop-methyl, atrazine, imidachloprid, benazolin, phosphinothricin, glyphosate, dipterex, chlorpyrifos, phoxim, avermectin, tribenuron-methyl, diafenthiuron, chlorfluazuron, and deltamethrin (Fig. 6d). Obviously, the response signal of CTEzyme to other pesticides, even organophosphorus pesticides (phosphinothricin, glyphosate, dipterex, chlorpyrifos, and phoxim) in high concentration (Figs. S10 and 6e), suggesting the specific interaction between MD and CTEzyme. In addition, a series of common pesticides were added into the assay systems containing MD to evaluate the anti-interference ability of the proposed assay method. As shown in Fig. S11, there was almost no change in the fluorescence ratio response, demonstrating the excellent anti-interference capability. The above results indicated that CTEzyme had excellent specificity for detecting MD, possibly because of the benign affinity between Cu in CTEzyme and sulfur in MD [62].

In addition, CTEzyme exhibited excellent long-term storage stability, maintaining over 99 % of the initial response for MD after 5 months (Fig. S12). To better simulate the actual assay conditions, the potential interfering ions (Ba2+, Ca2+, Ce2+, Co2+, Mn2+ and Na+) in high concentrations (1 mM) were also applied. The proposed method exhibited strong resistance against these coexistence of interfering metal ions (Fig. S13).

To further investigate the assay performance of the proposed method for real samples, tap water, river water and lake water were chosen as model samples (Table S2). The initial concentration of MD in these water samples was found to be below the detection limit using both the traditional high-performance liquid chromatography (Fig. S14) and the proposed method. Therefore, MD detection in the real water samples was conducted using the standard-addition method. The concentration values were calculated by the corresponding working curve (Fig. 6c). As shown in Table S2, the detection values of MD were in good accord with the spiked standard values, and the method showed good detection recovery (97.3105.7 %), suggesting good accuracy and reliability. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was within 2.6 %, suggesting excellent reproducibility and precision.

In summary, to resolve a series of problems about ratiometric fluorescence pesticide assay, we successfully developed a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on CTEzyme. Firstly, a CTEzyme was designed and synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method, which exhibited CTE properties and peroxidase-like activity. The peroxidase-like activity of CTEzyme was capable of catalyzing OPD to produce the fluorescent DAP in the presence of H2O2. MD could specifically inhibit the peroxidase-like activity of CTEzyme, causing the first fluorescence signal from the decrease of fluorescent DAP. MD could absorb on the CTEzyme to stable cluster structure, generating the second fluorescence signal from RIM and AIE effects. The two fluorescence signals are both induced by MD, which are relatively independent, improving the self-validation capacity and avoiding the interdependence of dual fluorescence signals in common ratiometric fluorescence assays. Therefore, the proposed assay method demonstrated high sensitivity, simplicity, cost-effectiveness, robust reliability for real sample assay. This work not only provides a ratiometric fluorescent assay method based on two independent signals triggered by targets, but also a low-cost specific recognition element for pesticide detection. The introduction of CTEzyme would broaden the range of available AIEzymes, and further propel the innovations in design and applications of AIEzymes.

Zhao Yanfang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. Chen Haiying: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Han Lei: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Chang Chao: Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xu Lubin: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ZR2024YQ026 and ZR2021MB018), Research Foundation for Distinguished Scholars of Qingdao Agricultural University (No. 663–1117015). We are grateful to staff from the Instrumental Analysis Center of Qingdao Agricultural University for providing valuable help.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2025.137491.

Hallam, Functionalised CuO nanostructures for the detection of organophosphorus pesticides: A non-enzymatic inhibition approach coupled with nano-scale electrode engineering to improve electrode sensitivity, Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem., 260 (2018) 480−489.

Rouillon, Electrochemical impedimetric sensor based on molecularly imprinted polymers/sol–gel chemistry for methidathion organophosphorous insecticide recognition, Talanta, 130 (2014) 294−298.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecular imprinting solid phase extraction for selective detection of methidathion in olive oil

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Bakas, N. Ben Oujji, E. Moczko, G. Istamboulie, S. Piletsky, E. Piletska, I. Ait-Ichou, E. Ait-Addi, T. Noguer, R. Rouillon, Molecular imprinting solid phase extraction for selective detection of methidathion in olive oil, Anal. Chim. Acta, 734 (2012) 99−105.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Inoue, T. Saito, H. Mase, Y. Suzuki, K. Takazawa, I. Yamamoto, S. Inokuchi, Rapid simultaneous determination for organophosphorus pesticides in human serum by LC–MS, J. Pharm. Biomed. Anal., 44 (2007) 258−264.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Evaluation of two extraction approaches for pesticide residue determination in biobeds using GC-MS/MS

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.S. Vareli, I.R. Pizzutti, L. Gebler, C.D. Cardoso, M.E.Z. Fontana, B. Reichert, B.D. Janisch, Evaluation of two extraction approaches for pesticide residue determination in biobeds using GC-MS/MS, Anal. Methods, 11 (2019) 5455−5463.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Single-atom iron containing nanozyme with peroxidase-like activity and copper nanoclusters based ratio fluorescent strategy for acetylcholinesterase activity sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Wang, L. Liu, X. Xie, X. Zhou, Z. Lin, X. Su, Single-atom iron containing nanozyme with peroxidase-like activity and copper nanoclusters based ratio fluorescent strategy for acetylcholinesterase activity sensing, Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem., 313 (2020) 128023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A heteronuclear W/Cu/S clusters-based donor–acceptor polymer for perovskite solar cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Yao, Z. Fang, H. Ren, X. Mu, G.B. Xiao, X. Zou, J. Cao, A Heteronuclear W/Cu/S Clusters-Based Donor–Acceptor Polymer for Perovskite Solar Cells, Adv. Funct. Mater., (2024) 2404671.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yanfang Zhao received her Ph.D. degree from the Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2006. Currently, he is an associate professor in the College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University. Her current research interests cover analytical chemistry, bioelectrochemistry and chemical biosensors.

Haiying Chen is currently working as a master student under the supervision of Prof. Lei Han in the College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, China. Her research interests focus on the nanozyme-based analytical method.

Chao Chang received his Master's degree under the supervision of Associate Prof. Yanfang Zhao and Prof. Lei Han in the College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, China. His research interests focus on the nanozyme-based analytical method.

Lubin Xu received his Ph.D. degree from the Zhejiang University (China) in 2006. Currently, he is an associate professor in the College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University. His current research interests cover organic synthesis and nanomaterials.

Lei Han received his PhD and Master's degree from University of Chinese Academy of Sciences. He Joined Qingdao Agricultural University in 2016, where he is currently a full professor in College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences. He was selected for two consecutive years in the World’s Top 2 % Scientists List for career-long impact. Her research interests include the exploration of new bio-nano-materials, new molecular recognition elements, and their applications on sensors and intelligent agriculture.
